title: Lifecycle Strategy for Log Retention
description:
## Hot for 30 Days, Then Cheap for 7 Years

An application writes log files to Amazon S3. The logs are **accessed heavily for the first 30 days** (debugging, dashboards), after which they are **rarely if ever read** but must be **retained for 7 years** for compliance, at the **lowest possible storage cost**. Occasional retrieval after 30 days is acceptable even if it takes hours. Which S3 strategy BEST meets these requirements?

options.text: An S3 Lifecycle policy that keeps objects in S3 Standard for 30 days, transitions them to S3 Glacier Deep Archive, and expires them after 7 years
Keep all logs in S3 Standard for the full 7 years to guarantee instant access
Move logs to S3 Standard-IA on day 1 and leave them there for 7 years
Delete logs after 30 days and re-generate them if an auditor ever asks

explanation: The access pattern is predictable — hot for 30 days, then cold for years with tolerance for hours-long retrieval — which is the ideal case for an **S3 Lifecycle policy**: serve fast/cheap reads from **S3 Standard** during the active window, then **transition to S3 Glacier Deep Archive** (the lowest-cost archival class, retrieval in hours) for the long compliance tail, and finally an **expiration action** deletes objects at 7 years. Keeping everything in **Standard** (B) is by far the most expensive for 7 years of rarely-read data. **Standard-IA from day 1** (C) is wrong twice: it penalizes the heavy first-30-day reads with retrieval fees and is still much pricier than Deep Archive for the long cold tail. **Deleting after 30 days** (D) violates the 7-year retention requirement (and logs are not reliably re-creatable).

hints.title: Known pattern = lifecycle rules
hints.body: When the cool-down timing is known (30 days), a lifecycle transition is better than letting a class auto-tier.

hints.title: Cheapest archival tier
hints.body: For data read maybe never but kept for years, with hours-acceptable retrieval, which class is the absolute cheapest at rest?
